learningGoal: How to spot obvious category groups like animals, fruits, colours, and sports — you'll get a feel for this really quickly!
How to find the 3-and-2 split where three words share a group and the other two are the odd ones out

screens.title: Which two don't belong?
screens.body:
Daisy is sorting five words into groups.

Here are five words — have a look:
**apple  •  carrot  •  banana  •  grape  •  potato**

Three of these belong together. The other two don't fit. Can you spot which two are the odd ones out? Trust your instincts!

screens.title: Find the group of 3
screens.body: Look at **apple, carrot, banana, grape, potato**. Here's the clever bit: don't hunt for the two odd ones. Instead, find the **three** words that connect — once you spot the group of 3, the odd pair reveals itself like magic!

interaction.sentence: In Odd Two Out, you need to find the group of ____ words that share a category
interaction.correctFeedback: Yes! Find the 3 that connect, and the leftover 2 are the odd ones out. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — you're looking for the group of 3 that share a category!

steps.text: Look at: apple, carrot, banana, grape, potato
steps.why: Scan all five words

steps.text: Group of 3: apple, banana, grape → all fruits
steps.why: These three share a clear category

steps.text: Odd two: carrot & potato → both vegetables
steps.why: These don't fit the main group ✓

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Which two words are the odd ones out?

interaction.question: Which pair are the odd two out?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! Apple, banana, and grape are all fruits. Carrot and potato are vegetables — they don't belong with the fruit group. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "carrot & potato". Apple, banana, and grape are all fruits. Carrot and potato are vegetables — they don't belong with the fruit group. ✓

visualProps.label: Find the 3 that share a category

screens.title: Concrete categories — you've got this!
screens.body: Nice work! Here's your recipe for obvious category splits — keep it simple:

steps.text: 1. Read all five words
steps.why: Don't jump at the first pair you see

steps.text: 2. Ask: what category do 3 of these share?
steps.why: Fruits? Animals? Colours? Sports?

steps.text: 3. The leftover 2 are the odd ones out
steps.why: They don't fit the main group ✓

learningGoal: How to spot less obvious groups like emotions, qualities, and weather types — these are the ones that trip people up!
Why abstract categories need a bit more digging than simple ones (and why that makes them more satisfying to crack)

screens.title: These words are trickier to group...
screens.body:
Evie is puzzling over a tricky word set.

**serene  •  furious  •  tranquil  •  placid  •  irate**

These aren't simple categories like "fruits" or "animals" — they're trickier! But that's what makes them interesting. You need to think about what each word actually **means** and how they **relate** to each other.

screens.title: Think about meaning, not appearance
screens.body: Look at **serene, furious, tranquil, placid, irate** — these are abstract (idea) words. Ask yourself: **what does each word describe?** Group by meaning, not by how they look or sound.

steps.text: Think: what does each word mean?
steps.why: Define each word in your head

steps.text: Group of 3: serene, tranquil, placid
steps.why: All words meaning calm and peaceful

steps.text: Odd two: furious & irate
steps.why: Both words meaning very angry ✓

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Which two words are the odd ones out?

interaction.question: Which pair are the odd two out?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! Serene, tranquil, and placid all mean calm and peaceful. Furious and irate both mean very angry — they are the odd two out. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "furious & irate". Serene, tranquil, and placid all mean calm and peaceful. Furious and irate both mean very angry — they are the odd two out. ✓

visualProps.label: Group by meaning, not by appearance

screens.title: Abstract categories — you can do these!
screens.body: Well done for digging deeper! When the category isn't obvious, here's your go-to plan:

steps.text: 1. Define each word in your head
steps.why: What does it actually mean?

steps.text: 2. Look for 3 words with a shared meaning
steps.why: Emotions? Qualities? Types of event?

steps.text: 3. Check the leftover 2 also share something
steps.why: If both leftovers connect, you've found the split ✓

learningGoal: How to group words by what things DO rather than what they ARE — this is a really clever way to think!
Why asking 'what is this used for?' can reveal hidden connections that aren't obvious at first

screens.title: What do these things DO?
screens.body:
Ruby is grouping words by their purpose.

**bucket  •  hammer  •  jug  •  vase  •  spanner**

Here's a sneaky twist: sometimes the connection isn't about what things ARE — it's about what they're **used for**. Imagine picking each thing up. What would you DO with it?

screens.title: Group by purpose
screens.body: Look at **bucket, hammer, jug, vase, spanner**. Ask yourself: **"What is each thing used for?"** Things with the same purpose belong together, even if they look completely different.

interaction.correctFeedback: Perfect! Group by purpose, not appearance. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — start by reading all words, then think about each one's purpose.

steps.text: Ask "what does each thing do?"
steps.why: Think about function, not appearance

steps.text: Group of 3: bucket, jug, vase
steps.why: All containers that hold things

steps.text: Odd two: hammer & spanner
steps.why: Both tools for fixing and building ✓

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Which two words are the odd ones out?

interaction.question: Which pair are the odd two out?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! Bucket, jug, and vase are all containers — they hold things inside them. Hammer and spanner are tools you use to fix or build, so they are the odd two out. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "hammer & spanner". Bucket, jug, and vase are all containers — they hold things inside them. Hammer and spanner are tools you use to fix or build, so they are the odd two out. ✓

visualProps.label: Think about what each is USED FOR

screens.title: Function categories — smart thinking!
screens.body: Brilliant! You've just learned to look beyond what things ARE and think about what they DO. When things look different but share a purpose:

steps.text: 1. Ask: what is each thing USED FOR?
steps.why: Holding? Cutting? Measuring? Moving?

steps.text: 2. Find 3 things with the same job
steps.why: Containers? Vehicles? Instruments?

steps.text: 3. The other 2 share a different purpose
steps.why: Different job = odd ones out ✓

learningGoal: How to group words by what things are made of: metals, fabrics, woods
Why material-based categories are a common 11+ trick

screens.title: What are these things made of?
screens.body:
Evie is spotting material-based groups.

**iron  •  cotton  •  steel  •  bronze  •  silk**

Did you know that material groups pop up all the time in the 11+? Things like metal, wood, stone, and fabric. The question is: **what are these things made of?**

screens.title: Group by material type
screens.body: Look at **iron, cotton, steel, bronze, silk**. Ask: **"What is each thing made of?"** or **"What type of material is this?"** Three will share a material type, two won't.

steps.text: What type of material is each word?
steps.why: Metal? Wood? Fabric? Stone?

steps.text: Group of 3: iron, steel, bronze
steps.why: All metals

steps.text: Odd two: cotton & silk
steps.why: Both fabrics ✓

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Which two words are the odd ones out?

interaction.question: Which pair are the odd two out?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! Iron, steel, and bronze are all metals. Cotton and silk are fabrics — they are the odd two out. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "cotton & silk". Iron, steel, and bronze are all metals. Cotton and silk are fabrics — they are the odd two out. ✓

screens.title: Material categories — handy knowledge!
screens.body: Great work! Knowing your material groups gives you a real advantage. Here are the common ones that come up in the 11+:

steps.text: Metals: iron, steel, bronze, copper, tin, aluminium
steps.why: Hard, shiny, mined from the ground

steps.text: Woods: oak, pine, willow, beech, ash, mahogany
steps.why: Come from trees

steps.text: Fabrics: cotton, silk, denim, wool, tweed, linen
steps.why: Woven or knitted materials ✓

learningGoal: How to spot sub-category tricks: all 5 words seem to belong, but only 3 share a tighter group — this is a favourite 11+ trick!
Why the obvious big category is actually a trap, and how to zoom in on the smaller sub-group

screens.title: They ALL seem to belong...
screens.body:
Daisy is facing a tricky sub-category question.

**eagle  •  sparrow  •  bat  •  penguin  •  robin**

At first glance, all five look like they belong together — they're all flying creatures! But here's where it gets interesting. Look closer, and you'll see that three of them share a **tighter** group within the bigger category.

screens.title: Find the tighter sub-group
screens.body: All five words — **eagle, sparrow, bat, penguin, robin** — are flying creatures. But don't stop at the big category! Ask: **"Can I split these further?"** The sub-group of 3 is the real answer.

steps.text: Big category: all flying creatures
steps.why: This is the TRAP — too broad!

steps.text: Tighter group of 3: eagle, sparrow, robin
steps.why: All common flying birds

steps.text: Odd two: bat & penguin
steps.why: not typical flying birds (bat is a mammal, penguin can't fly) ✓

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: All five are flying creatures — but which two are the odd ones out?

interaction.question: Which pair are the odd two out?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! Eagle, sparrow, and robin are all common flying birds. Bat is a mammal (not a bird at all), and penguin is a bird that can't fly. Both are odd ones out. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "bat & penguin". Eagle, sparrow, and robin are all common flying birds. Bat is a mammal (not a bird at all), and penguin is a bird that can't fly. Both are odd ones out. ✓

screens.title: Sub-category trick — now you know!
screens.body: Excellent detective work! When all 5 words seem to fit one group, that's your signal to dig deeper:

steps.text: 1. If all 5 fit one big category, that's the TRAP
steps.why: The question always has 2 odd ones out!

steps.text: 2. Split the big category into smaller groups
steps.why: Fish vs shellfish? Flowers vs trees? Strings vs percussion?

steps.text: 3. The group of 3 is the tighter sub-category
steps.why: The other 2 belong to a different sub-group ✓

learningGoal: How to find the 3 that CONNECT rather than hunting for the 2 that are odd — this changes everything!
Why starting with the group of 3 is faster, more reliable, and way less stressful

screens.title: Don't hunt for the odd ones — find the THREE!
screens.body:
Evie is learning the best strategy for odd-two-out.

**fork  •  saucepan  •  knife  •  spoon  •  frying pan**

Here's a game-changer: most people try to spot the 2 "weird" words, but that's actually the hard way round! It's much easier to find the **3 that connect** — then the other 2 are automatically the odd ones out. Let's try it!

screens.title: The 'Find Three' method
screens.body:
Let's try it with **fork, saucepan, knife, spoon, frying pan**.

Step 1: Scan all 5 words.
Step 2: Find 3 that share a clear category.
Step 3: The leftover 2 are your answer.

This is faster and more reliable than trying to spot oddness!

steps.text: Scan: fork, saucepan, knife, spoon, frying pan
steps.why: Read all five — don't rush

steps.text: Three that connect: fork, knife, spoon
steps.why: All cutlery

steps.text: Leftover two: saucepan & frying pan
steps.why: Both cooking pans — done! ✓

screens.title: Your turn — find the three!
screens.body: Find the 3 that connect — the other 2 are the odd ones out.

interaction.question: Which pair are the odd two out?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! Fork, knife, and spoon are all cutlery (things you eat with). Saucepan and frying pan are cooking pans — they are the odd two out. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "saucepan & frying pan". Fork, knife, and spoon are all cutlery (things you eat with). Saucepan and frying pan are cooking pans — they are the odd two out. ✓

screens.title: Find Three — the golden rule
screens.body: You've just learned the fastest strategy for Odd Two Out questions. This will save you loads of time in the exam:

steps.text: DON'T hunt for 2 odd words
steps.why: That's slow and unreliable

steps.text: DO find the 3 that share a category
steps.why: Once you spot the 3, the 2 are obvious

steps.text: Check: do the leftover 2 also share something?
steps.why: If yes, you've definitely got it right ✓

learningGoal: How to handle odd-two-out when the words look scary: literary, scientific, and musical terms
Why knowing a few key vocabulary sets gives you a massive advantage (you'll recognise more than you think!)

screens.title: Don't panic — use what you know!
screens.body:
Daisy is facing advanced vocabulary in her VR paper.

**simile  •  adjective  •  metaphor  •  personification  •  adverb**

Some of these words might look a bit scary at first. But here's the thing — you probably know more of them than you think! Even recognising **one** grouping can unlock the whole answer.

screens.title: Use what you DO know
screens.body: Look at **simile, adjective, metaphor, personification, adverb**. You don't need to know every word — if you can spot **2 or 3 words** from the same subject area, you can work out the grouping.

steps.text: Spot any words you recognise as a group
steps.why: Even 2 from the same subject is enough to start

steps.text: Group of 3: simile, metaphor, personification
steps.why: All figures of speech (literary techniques)

steps.text: Odd two: adjective & adverb
steps.why: Both word classes (parts of speech) ✓

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body:
Which two words are the odd ones out?

Hint: Think about which words you might use in an English lesson about writing techniques.

interaction.question: Which pair are the odd two out?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! Simile, metaphor, and personification are all figures of speech (literary devices). Adjective and adverb are word classes — they are the odd two out. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "adjective & adverb". Simile, metaphor, and personification are all figures of speech (literary devices). Adjective and adverb are word classes — they are the odd two out. ✓

visualProps.label: Use words you recognise to find the group

screens.title: Hard vocabulary — don't panic, you've got this!
screens.body: Brilliant effort! When words look scary, just remember: you don't need to know every single word. Here's your plan:

steps.text: 1. Find any words you DO recognise
steps.why: You probably know at least 2-3 of them

steps.text: 2. Group those known words — what subject are they from?
steps.why: Science? English? Music? Geography?

steps.text: 3. The unfamiliar words often belong to the OTHER group
steps.why: Your known words reveal the split ✓

learningGoal: How to avoid being tricked by words that LOOK like they belong together but actually don't — the 11+ loves this trick!
Why your first instinct can sometimes lead you astray, and how to double-check before committing

screens.title: Watch out — this one's sneaky!
screens.body:
Evie is spotting a clever trap in a word puzzle.

**sand  •  shore  •  tide  •  wave  •  dune**

You might think sand, shore, and dune go together (all 'beachy' things). But is that really the best grouping? Sometimes the most obvious connection is a **red herring** — a deliberate trap designed to catch you out! Let's see if you can spot the real split.

screens.title: Test EVERY possible grouping
screens.body: With **sand, shore, tide, wave, dune**, don't lock onto the first connection you see. Try different groupings and pick the one where **all 3 clearly belong** and the **2 leftovers also connect**.

steps.text: First thought might be wrong — test it!
steps.why: You might think sand, shore, and dune go together (all 'beachy' things)

steps.text: Better group of 3: shore, tide, wave
steps.why: All things to do with the sea and water movement

steps.text: Odd two: sand & dune
steps.why: Both things made of sand (land features, not water) ✓

screens.title: Your turn — don't fall for the trap!
screens.body: Which two words are the odd ones out? Be careful — one grouping is a trap!

interaction.question: Which pair are the odd two out?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! Shore, tide, and wave are all connected to the sea and water movement. Sand and dune are both land features made of sand — they look 'beachy' but aren't about water. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "sand & dune". Shore, tide, and wave are all connected to the sea and water movement. Sand and dune are both land features made of sand — they look 'beachy' but aren't about water. ✓

visualProps.label: Test every grouping — the obvious one might be wrong!

screens.title: Red herrings — you won't fall for these!
screens.body: Superb! Now you know about red herrings, you're much harder to trick. Watch out for words with double meanings — they're the biggest traps:

steps.text: 1. Don't jump at the first grouping you see
steps.why: The obvious connection might be the trap

steps.text: 2. Check: do the 2 leftovers also share something?
steps.why: If the leftovers DON'T connect, try a different split

steps.text: 3. Words with 2 meanings (pupil, spring, castle) are common traps
steps.why: Always consider BOTH meanings of a word ✓
